data.title: Viking Holdings: Valuation Is Rich
data.description:
Investment overview

I wrote about Viking Holdings (NYSE:VIK) previously with a hold rating, as I was still worried about the FY26 outlook, in particular the pricing trends and net yield growth. While the latest 2Q25 results were strong, the core question remains around FY26 pricing momentum. Bookings are clearly healthy, but with net yield growth still trailing historical levels, I see limited scope for further multiple expansion until there is clearer evidence of re-acceleration.

2Q25 earnings

VIK’s 2Q25 results showed the strength. Total revenue grew a staggering 18.5% y/y to $1.88 billion, driven by both higher capacity and pricing power. Capacity increased 8.8% y/y on fleet growth, while net yield increased 8% y/y. The Ocean segment was noteworthy, delivering a 12% y/y net yield gain in 1H25. Meanwhile, the River segment, which had shown weakness in 1Q25, rebounded meaningfully as sailing season ramped up, with 1H25 net yield up 6.9% y/y. Strong revenue momentum translated into earnings growth, with adj. EBITDA up 28.5% y/y to $633 million and adj. EPS up to $0.99.

Demand Outlook Strengthens, Easing Prior Concerns

The outlook for the current year has improved considerably since my last update. Back then (in May), VIK had already de-risked most of FY25 with 92% of capacity sold, but it surely doesn’t hurt to know that management has managed to push that figure to 96%, which basically means VIK has effectively sold out for the year. When combined with the pricing (net yield) that continues to run ~7% y/y higher, this level of pre-booking has essentially removed virtually all uncertainty I had for FY25, and in fact, demonstrated that consumer demand remains strong.

More importantly, the 2Q25 results also address the “lack of visibility” that I was worried about in May. To be specific, VIK's booked position (percentage of total available cruise capacity that has already been sold for a future season at a specific point in time) for the 2026 season has surged to 55% of capacity, which is a very big improvement from the 37% figure seen in 1Q25. I remind readers that this increase is based on an even larger capacity (9% y/y capacity growth), which means VIK is not only selling further ahead but also successfully filling an even larger fleet well in advance. The strength becomes even clearer when viewed in dollar terms. Advanced bookings for 2026 now stand at $3.9 billion, representing a 13% increase vs. the same point last year for the 2025 season. This shows that top-line demand is growing at a double-digit clip and is easily absorbing new supply. The latter is important because it justifies VIK’s expansion strategy. Management commentary was also very encouraging. They mentioned that June and July saw outstanding levels of bookings, with the momentum continuing into August. In my view, this does suggest that the demand surge is not a blip since it has been sustained through the peak selling window.

Shifting focus to the River segment, there has also been an obvious turnaround success. After a soft start earlier in the year, advanced bookings per passenger cruise days [PCD] for FY26 are now pacing at ~6% y/y. This is a sharp turnaround and points to pricing power returning as the booking curve for the main European sailing season matures (i.e., as consumers finalize plans and book their trips). Note: The European sailing season is between April and October.

FY26 Pricing Remains the Key Question Mark

Despite the encouraging booking volumes, the key issue I highlighted previously (the softer pricing trend for FY26) is still bugging me, and I think that puts a limit to the upside. For perspective, overall advanced bookings per PCD for 2026 are tracking at +4% y/y, essentially unchanged from May. This is well below the ~7% pricing growth VIK had achieved for FY25 at the same stage. This is also below VIK’s historical net yield trajectory. While management reiterated confidence in ultimately reaching mid-single-digit price increases for the year, the lack of visible momentum (pricing still at ~4% y/y) leaves me concerned that strong volumes are coming at the expense of softer pricing.

In fact, if you look deeper, the flat 4% overall pricing figure masks two contrasting trends. Firstly, river pricing has actually accelerated, but this was offset by a slowdown in the ocean segment. For ocean cruises, advanced bookings per PCD eased to 3.7% y/y, down from 5.0% in my previous update. Relative to River, Ocean is a much bigger segment (VIK main growth driver), and hence, this makes the entire pricing concern of mine even more important.

My belief is that this creates a new layer of complexity/uncertainty. Even as management suggested they could have been more aggressive on pricing, the moderation signals otherwise. This comes at a time when VIK is showing good booking strength. If VIK tries to raise prices and booking momentum stalls, it will very likely strike fear in investors that volume is indeed growing at the expense of pricing.

As such, until there is clearer evidence that the FY26 pricing curve is re-accelerating back toward the historical algorithm, I remain cautious.

Valuation

Anyway, even if I thought the fundamentals were great and wanted to upgrade to buy, VIK’s valuation has soared to levels beyond my imagination. Since May, the forward EBITDA multiple has surged to 15x (from low teens). Inserting that valuation into my previous model (anchored against FY26 consensus EBITDA estimates), this implies a share price target of $58, which is near where VIK is trading today. I think it's hard to imagine valuation going any higher from here since this is near the all-time high and is at near the widest margin vs. peers’ valuation (peers include Royal Caribbean Cruises, Carnival Corp, and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings). Hence, EBITDA needs to go up to justify a higher share price, but that is where I am uncertain. If pricing continues to decelerate with bookings growth, it limits VIK’s ability to accelerate growth. Therefore, I am still not willing to upgrade to a buy.

Conclusion

I maintain a hold rating for VIK. The near-term setup is strong, with FY25 essentially sold out, FY26 bookings ahead of pace, and River segment pricing improving. However, the lack of acceleration in consolidated FY26 pricing, particularly in the Ocean segment, still makes me cautious. Coupled with a valuation now at ~15x forward EBITDA—well above peers and near historical peaks—I believe risk/reward remains balanced.

data.title: Light At The End Of The AI Bottleneck: Pre-Commercial Poet Technologies Stock Is Buyable
data.description:
Numbers, usually an investor’s best friend, can also become an obstacle.

To a Seeking Alpha investor audience, I’d be preaching to the choir if I explained the importance of numbers (relating to earnings, financial condition, etc.) such as those reported regularly by publicly traded companies.

I’ve been screening for stocks since the 1980s, when the program was on a floppy disk in the pc’s A drive. Data, stored on a second floppy, went into the B drive. It was cool to watch the A- and B-drive lights flash back and forth as the computer read alternately from the program and the data.

(Footnote for history buffs or trivia fans: When hard drives were added, they were labeled C. Floppy disks are now long gone. This is why computers today have a C drive (and D, etc. if one programmatically partitions them), but nothing for A or B.)

I even published two books, one on fundamentals-based screening and the other on valuation. (I’m not telling you to buy them – they might be a bit dated by now.)

I say this so you understand who is speaking here as I, a long-time fundamental value guy and quant, tell you, as I have for other early stage companies, that I’m going to rate shares of POET Technologies (NASDAQ: POET), a pre-commercial photonics company that adds light (photonics) to conventional semiconductor-based electronic transmission, a “Buy.”

I’m not going to address Graham and Dodd, Warren Buffett, or anybody like that (although I’m well-studied in all of them).

Instead, I’ll cite an important guru you may not know of. His name is Craveth Read. He was an 18th century British logician. His famous adage (often erroneously attributed to John Maynard Keynes) is as follows:

“[I]t is a mistake to aim at an unattainable precision. It is better to be vaguely right than exactly wrong.”

Investment analysis of POET based on any discussion of current or historical numbers for is “exactly wrong.” Such considerations aim at “unattainable precision.”

That’s so for pre-commercial POET. It’s also so for minimal startup-level revenue companies. We see these in quantum computing companies, and many in the biotech field.

This does not mean we should plunge wily nilly into any such company. Analysis is still critical. But we need a different approach, a qualitative analysis.

It’s how value-guru William Miller chose to invest big in Amazon (AMZN) stock almost from day one.

That said…

For Those Who Crave Ratios, I’ll Invent One Right Now: S/R

S stands for Story.

This can be about technology. It can be about a new business model. It can be anything like this. The key is that it be big and credible.

R stands for Risk.

This isn’t about risk in the abstract. All such companies, by their incipient nature, are at the highest level of the risk scale. It’s really about your personal risk tolerance.

For you, R is a two-part question.

  1. Do you want to assume this kind of risk at all?
  2. If you are willing to assume such risk, think about position sizing. It would likely be very bad to put 25% of your portfolio in a stock like POET. A stake below 5% (perhaps closer to 1%-2%) would seem more appropriate for many investors.

Given that risk-tolerance is personal to you, I’ll focus here on Story.

Introducing POET – What It’s About and What It Does

“Let there be light,” pertained (biblically speaking) to the universe eons ago.

POET is now bringing it to semiconductors — chips, those electronic gizmos developed in the mid-20th century and with which we now can’t seem to live without.

Semiconductors make much of today’s technology possible. Lots of data and/or logic are crammed onto these tiny things. They involve etching complex pathways onto thin wafers. Electrical-conducting substances go into those pathways. So, too, do itty bitty logic processors, and gizmos to prevent them from overheating.

Absent semiconductors, who knows how much voluminous wiring etc. we’d need to in order to bulk up even the simplest among todays’ devices.

If we could do everything imaginable with electrical current, we’d be golden — and POET would not exist or it would be doing something very different from what it actually does. But…

Light (Photonics) Has Properties Electronics Can’t Match

The semiconductor folks are quite candid about things photonics can do that traditional semiconductors can’t. Semiengineering.com tells us what’s great about photonics:

* Speed: Nothing travels faster than photons, so using light pulses to transmit information is ideal for speed-centric applications.

* Bandwidth: The characteristics of light (wide frequency range, multiple wavelengths, minimal interference, low signal loss) enable immense amounts of information to be transmitted at once.

* Energy: Photonics-based systems are typically very energy efficient due to the minimal loss of signal over long distances.

* Size: Photonic components like sensors, microLEDs, waveguides, and integrated circuits can be made highly compact to minimize the physical footprint of various technologies.

Interesting list. But it’s not qualitatively surprising. These all sound like areas in which modern chip-based electronics are superior to pre-semiconductor electrical transmission.

So, you may rightfully ask, why do we need photonics. Why can’t we just make electronic chips even more powerful?

I think it boils down to the reality that electricity (based on movement of electrons) and light (based on movement of protons) are two different things each with its own set of properties.

For more, we’d have to ask the creator of the universe why it was set up that way. Since I am not the creator, nor in direct communication with said being, I’ll have to stop here and leave it at that. Put another way…

It’s the creator’s universe, and we just live in it — and cope as best we can.

And if we are gong to do our best, we’ll need to incorporate photonics to enhance modern electronics. We need to call on light to take us where electronics alone can’t go.

That’s hard, because…

From a Manufacturing Standpoint, Photonics is Much More Demanding Than Electronics

As challenging as it can be to put metallic circuits onto etched semiconductor wafers, etc., photonics is harder.

You see, electronic signals can move just as easily through all kinds of pathways. But light wants to travel in straight lines. That means waveguides (analogous to electronic circuits) have to work harder to direct photonic “currents” to go where they need to go.

Photonic circuits must be aligned with extreme precision, more so than already precise (by normal people standards) electronic chips.

Electronic signals can be amplified. Light signals cannot. So, with photonics you get one chance to get the right intensity.

Photonics is far more temperature sensitive than electronics (the latter’s sensitivity already seems quite high to us mortals).

There are also differences between electrical and optical communication methods, production costs, and commercial scalability.

This sounds frustrating. It seems as if we can’t do our best to work with both electronics and light unless we take on a lot of extra cost and hassle.

How good would it be if we could work with light as we do with electronics, or if we could “semiconductorize” photonics, to produce light-handling gizmos at the wafer-scale we’ve come to know and love with semiconductors.

Good news!

It looks like we’re getting there…

POET’s Optical Interposer Aims at Heretofore Unprecedented Electronics-Photonics Integration

I’ve written much here extolling the virtues and challenges of combining electronics with photonics. Given all that, you’d have to expect that many companies – some of them quite large – would be working hard at this.

You’d be right. This is a busy area.

Companies in one aspect of this or another include Coherent (COHR), Cisco (CSCO), Intel (INTC), Broadcom (AVGO), and, Innolight (a Chinese company).

Wow. Would we want a pre-commercial company that has to compete with outfits like those? I wouldn’t. And I’m not suggesting you buy into any. POET isn’t actually a competitor to any of them.

POET is an enabler. Its optical interposer is actually a platform. Optical engines and transceivers, the things those companies make, are built from a platform such as the one POET is developing.

Phew. We can breathe now – or can we?

POET is not the only company working on such platforms. But here, at least, the rivals aren’t gigantic household names. But pros in the field know of them. Some names (none publicly traded) include Celestial AI, Ayar Labs, and Ranovus.

Ultimately, though, it’s not necessarily about big-name companies. As technology progresses, new names enter the field. And right now, POET has entered in a strong way.

There is one thing unique about POET. But it’s a big one…

It boils down to a label POET rightfully adopted for itself – “semiconductorization” of photonics. It’s about the way POET builds its platform.

The company…

integrates lasers, detectors, and electronic circuits all on a single chip-level module. This design could slash the cost and power consumption of optical transceivers used in data centers and telecom. POET’s technology aims to eliminate many of the traditional assembly steps. Rather than individually wiring components, Optical Interposer has them all “printed” together. The company has started sampling 100G/200G/400G fiber links and has a joint venture in Asia for manufacturing. If POET secures design wins with big network gear makers or cloud providers, it could scale up revenue quickly from a small base.

POET puts it this way:

The POET Optical Interposer™ utilizes a novel waveguide technology that allows the integration of electronic and photonic devices into a single multi-chip module. By applying advanced wafer-level semiconductor manufacturing techniques and novel packaging methods, POET’s Optical Interposer eliminates costly components, assembly, alignment and testing methods employed in conventional photonics solutions. In addition to lowering costs compared to conventional devices….

Interposers have been used for several years to allow electronic devices (microprocessors, memory devices, etc.) to communicate with each other via high-speed metal traces embedded in a semiconductor wafer. POET invented methods for embedding or placing photonic devices or features (lasers, detectors, waveguides, filters, etc.) within the interposer, allowing high-speed communication between electronic and photonic devices.

The POET Optical Interposer consists of layers of materials built on a standard semiconductor wafer of any size, using standard semiconductor (CMOS) processes. The bottom layers include the high-speed metal traces found in electronic interposers, while the top-most layers incorporate waveguides that guide and focus light, other passive devices (e.g., filters and gratings) and features which enable the accurate placement and integration of active devices.

Don’t sweat the details if you aren’t a hard-core tech person.

Here’s another, graphic, view.

Ultimately, you should focus on the overall themes of integration and easier manufacturing.

That’s POET’s unique calling card!

And as calling cards go, that’s a great one to have…

Why the World Needs What POET is Working to Supply

This is easy.

How often have you heard thoughtful commentators taking, and worrying, about the enormous power needs of AI? (And let’s add in quantum computing, crypto, factory automation, etc.)

We want things done quickly, incredibly quickly, so quickly we can’t even count the nanoseconds.

Occasional latency as you try to start a Netflix (NFLX) program can make it feel like steam is coming out of your ears.

Now imagine even tinier latency as a robo-taxi is trying to decide whether it can make safely make a left turn (across oncoming traffic) on a busy New York City two-way avenue. No, no, no to latency.

If you’re among those who think we’re over-investing in AI, data centers, etc. and that we’re already at or near where we need to be…

Seriously, try to find a time machine and hitch a ride back to, say, 1985.

Check out the supercomputers and super-networks of the era. I still remember a back-in-the-day analyst meeting with Cablevision, the cable TV firm that was acquired by Altice USA (ATUS) in 2016. I’m not sure who chaired the meeting, possibly the firm’s late founder Charles Dolan.

My most vivid memory was of Dolan (or whoever) suggesting it may not ever be possible to deliver movies to viewers to view when they wish because nobody would want to wait two hours or so for it to download. Maybe while you’re visiting the 1980s, you might try to sneak into that meeting!

Then, come home and do some AI stuff. (Try asking Perplexity AI to help you find good web authorities on complicated topics, as I often do.) Suddenly, you’ll notice it’s really slow, even the basic stuff. The more complex the task (for Perplexity, it could be Deep Research or Labs), the longer it takes. I rarely have the patience to use Labs.

Do you use AI for imaging or coding. Is it as fast as you want it to be? Reallly?

And in my 8/8/25 PLTR report, I described a simple inference experiment I did that shows how primitive today’s AI really is.

We’ve got bottlenecks that need clearing!

Folks, between continuing to develop AI, along with crypto etc., we need a scary amount of additional power. We need it at reasonable cost. And we need things to not overheat.

We need electronics and photonics to do more than just work together. We need them to work together well. And POET’s technology – its optical interposer – figures to play a big role in bursting through the bottlenecks that threaten to obstruct the progress we want and need.

Commercialization – Not Yet, But Prospects Coming into Focus

POET’s revenues aren’t quite at zero. But they’re darned close.

Sometimes the company will book modest revenues from non-recurring engineering services. Commercial revenue — from selling products — is actually zero.

But commercialization is getting closer. It’s not a snap-one’s-fingers now-we’re-doing-it thing. It’s a process.

POET is now focused on working with potential customers showing and qualifying its offerings, and establishing manufacturing arrangements.

Here’s how POET will fit into the overall marketplace:

This is a hard pill for many investors to swallow. We still use the future tense when discussing commerce.

But we know demand is strong.

So once commerce gets going, it’s likely to ramp up very quickly.

The Only Current Numbers Worth Even Barely Worth Looking At

At this stage in POET’s history, numbers are relevant for just one thing, to show whether or not POET is solvent.

The balance sheet shows the answer is “yes.”

A harder question is whether it can remain solvent going forward. It’ll take time for commercial revenues to catch up to and eventually surpass current and future expenses.

Every early stage company faces this same issue. And I give the same answer each time…

Capital markets will continue to supply as much as the company needs for as long as it needs it — so long as the markets believe in the viability and credibility of the business plans. I discuss this further in my 7/14/25 report on gene-editor CRSP.

The capital markets are showing themselves quite willing to support POET’s strategy. (See, for example, here, here, here, here, and here.)

Risk

As discussed above, POET is all the way at the top of the risk scale.

Being pre-commercial, the company stays alive thanks to its ability to have continually attracted outside capital. Such companies do so, and keep going like this, for as long as the capital markets believe in the credibility of the story.

POET is not alone in this. Much of biotech lives this way. A lot of AI firms still depend on outside capital. Quantum computing depends entirely on it.

Once upon a time, such companies wouldn’t even have had publicly traded stock. They’d have depended entirely on angel and/or venture capital financing.

That’s not so today. Now, many early stage investors are eager to cash out via IPO. And the equity markets are very accommodating.

We can like this. We can hate this.

No matter – This is how the world now works. All we can do is live in it, and decide for ourselves whether or not we wish to bear the risks of participating in this aspect of the equity market.

If POET’s technology doesn’t work, I can’t say what, if anything, would be left of the company.

What to do About POET Stock

This section is addressed ONLY to those who have made personal decisions to participate in POET’s portion of the risk scale. (I’m now talking to readers, who, if they reject the POET story, might wind up choosing a different comparably risky opportunity.)

Here’s the price chart. Treat this as a record showing how other risk-tolerant investors have been reacting to the POET story.

I’ve seen better. I’ve seen worse.

The 10-day exponential moving average (EMA) has been all over the place relative to the 50-day EMA. And the 10 just crossed below the 50. Not good. But it looks to be turning upward. So it may soon go back above the 50. For how long? Who knows.

Chaikin Money Flow (CMF) and the Chaikin Oscillator (CO) have often given mixed signals.

Both indicators measure which party to trades is more motivated. CMF does it for institutional investors. CO does it for the market in general.

At this moment, CO is bullish. CMF is not. Both indicators bounced around over the past year. But CMF has spent more time in conspicuously bearish territory. CO, when it’s been bearish, has been only modestly so. More often, CO has ranged between neutral and positive.

My takeaway from CMF and CO: POET, for now, seems more an individual investor stock than something the big institutions love. But that’s understandable. The latter likely struggle more with pre-commercial situations.

If I were an institutional investor, POET would likely appeal to me more between 5pm and 9am, when I’m on my own time and investing for myself.

Between 9am and 5pm (or whatever hours one works), I’d be in a professional fiduciary capacity investing other people’s money. That would likely give me pause, unless I’m working on a very specialized high-risk-tolerant account.

Lets face it. Sometimes, individual investors enjoy advantages over institutions!

When it comes to rating stocks, I think in terms of probable future stock performance relative to the market, rather than literally buying, holding or selling.

And since I’m not rating based on a quant model, I’ll eschew “Strong …” extremes. Absent an exceptional degree of conviction, I think “Buy,” Hold,” or “Sell” are enough.

I see POET as likely outperforming the broader 3- to 5-year market, and maybe even a year-ahead market if its starts commercializing as quickly as it now expects. (That could really wake up the institutions and do wonders for CMF.)

Also, be aware that Seeking Alpha Analyst Ahmed Abdelazim suggested on 8/21/25 that POET might become a takeover target. I don’t know. But I certainly won’t argue against that possibility.

As, essentially, a technology shop, I could easily see POET appealing to a larger company looking to buy rather than build (as many nowadays prefer). That’s especially so for POET considering its bottleneck-busting position within AI, etc.

I translate all this to the Seeking Alpha rating taxonomy by rating POET as a ‘Buy’ for readers whose personal risk tolerances allow them to consider pre-commercial emerging tech.

data.title: FTHY: Worth Monitoring For Term Opportunities
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

First Trust High Yield Opportunities 2027 Term (NYSE:FTHY) is a fixed-income focused fund, mixing in senior loans and high yield bonds. In our prior update, we noted that the discount had narrowed, and that started to make it less appealing. A year later, the discount has narrowed even a touch further. That would mean that I'd continue to see it as a 'Hold' here, though with its liquidation set in around two years, there could still be an opportunity to hold or monitor this fund more closely.

FTHY Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: 0.74
  • Discount/Premium: -3.05%
  • Distribution Yield: 10.26%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.80%
  • Leverage: 22.61%
  • Managed Assets: $716.481 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation around August 1st, 2027)

FTHY's investment objective is "to provide current income." They intend to do this by:

investing at least 80% of its Managed Assets in high yield debt securities of any maturity that are rated below investment grade at the time of purchase or unrated securities determined by the Advisor to be of comparable quality. High-yield debt securities include the U.S. and non-U.S. corporate debt obligations and senior, secured floating-rate loans ("Senior Loans").

This fund is leveraged, and that increases the fund's total expense ratio as the borrowings come with interest that needs to be paid. The fairly high operating expense gets pushed even higher to 3.26%. These borrowings are based on SOFR plus 1.05%, so they will see fluctuations as the Fed moves its benchmark rate.

On the other hand, given the fund's senior loan exposure, most of the fluctuations in the costs are absorbed. In other words, as rates rose, the underlying yield generated from the portfolio also increased, and vice versa.

Interestingly, they have taken their borrowings up since our prior update. They've even taken up the borrowings a bit since their latest annual report, which reflects the fiscal year ended May 31, 2025.

Discount Continues To Narrow

With FTHY being a fixed-income-oriented portfolio, it can make it more appropriate to compare its performance against fixed-income-focused ETFs as a barometer for how it has performed since our prior update. In this case, with its hybrid approach, the iShares iBoxx $ High Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) and the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN). These are non-leveraged ETFs that can help provide some overall context to how FTHY is performing. In this case, the fund's total returns have outpaced both of those ETFs.

However, the fund's total NAV returns were a bit lower. The NAV per share declined a hair from $15.49 to $15.01, but the fund also paid $1.64 in distributions during this time. That would have brought its returns to positive territory.

However, in the last year, the fund's discount narrowed, so that's how we know the fund's total NAV returns would have been slightly less. We were sitting at a discount of -4.52%, and it has narrowed to -3.05%. In fact, the fund's discount has narrowed quite materially from where it was only a couple of years ago. In the last three years, the average discount comes in at -8.13%.

This could be partially because we have the anticipated liquidation date coming up in just under two years now. That is, the date the fund is set to liquidate because it is a term structured fund. First Trust has been exiting the CEF business, converting funds into ETFs, or divesting some funds to Aberdeen.

With that, I suspect they'll let this fund liquidate as expected or potentially try to get a vote to convert to an ETF. Something that other fund sponsors tend to do with their term structured funds, particularly Nuveen and Western Asset, is to convert them into perpetual funds. It makes sense. Why would they want to give up fee-generating AUM?

However, they generally do so with a tender offer at 100% of NAV for 100% of shares outstanding, which allows investors to get out of the funds if they choose. Some, those that have traded at premiums for regular periods of time, have put votes up to shareholders to switch to perpetual. That can also make sense, because when trading at a premium, one wouldn't want to liquidate, as it would mean an immediate loss.

Either way, investors should be able to realize the actual discount in this fund. That's why this fund would be worth holding or monitoring more closely for outsized opportunities such as the April discount that gapped wider before narrowing sharply.

The liquidation and realization of the fund's discount can provide guaranteed alpha relative to a non-term structured fund that invested similarly. Of course, that does not mean guaranteed returns; this is because the fund's underlying portfolio could perform weakly with NAV erosion that the distributions may not be able to offset.

Double-Digit Distribution Appealing But Regular Adjustments

FTHY helps to deliver a relatively high distribution yield, coming in at 10.26%, pushing it just into the double-digit rate level. On a NAV basis, the fund's distribution rate comes to 9.99%, so quite similar given the fairly negligible discount these days. That said, since the fund's existence, we've seen quite a few adjustments in the payout.

That isn't necessarily uncommon for a fund exposed to floating rate exposure in its underlying portfolio, but FTHY doesn't have that much in terms of senior loans—mostly enough, it would seem, to offset the borrowing cost fluctuations.

In looking at the fund's financial highlights, we can see that net investment income for the last few years has been fairly stable. In 2021 and 2022, it was quite a bit higher, and that could explain some of the increased payout they probably felt comfortable doing. Still, the fund has never really covered its distribution entirely from NII. In fact, it has always been rather low for a fixed-income fund.

The latest shows around 57% distribution coverage, similar to what we saw in the last two years. Even in the fiscal 2022 year, where NII was highest, relative to what they paid out during that period would have put NII coverage at ~69%.

This is where the NAV erosion can come in, as they overdistribute. It has also come in with a higher rate environment, pushing their underlying portfolio lower.

Given the lack of coverage in the distribution, it isn't too surprising to see return of capital make up a rather hefty portion of the distribution tax classification for the last couple of years.

FTHY's Portfolio

One place the fund could earn the distribution would be from its underlying portfolio, potentially experiencing appreciation. While the junk-rated securities that FTHY invests in are relatively less interest rate sensitive, they aren't entirely immune. The weighted average effective duration comes to 3.23 years. When rates change 1% up or down, the fund would be expected to move by 3.23% up or down.

Only 2.22% of the portfolio is rated as investment-grade, and at BBB- it is just barely.

In looking at average YTM, it also suggests that even with the expected upside of receiving par back at maturity, the 'yield' here wouldn't be enough. That's assuming they held every single position to maturity and there were no defaults or bankruptcies from the underlying holdings.

This means that while the fund delivers a strong distribution yield that might be appealing, we would expect to continue to see the NAV erode a bit going forward. That is another reason why we would like to see a wider discount before we'd consider this a 'Buy' again.

That said, if rates decline meaningfully in the next two years, that could help to limit some of the NAV erosion or even eliminate it, as the underlying portfolio could see some appreciation.

At the same time, if we are getting meaningful rate cuts, it could suggest that it is because we are in an economic slowdown. That would likely cause its lower credit quality-rated portfolio to see more defaults and bankruptcies. That, in turn, likely means that less of the portfolio would experience that face value payoff at maturity.

High yield spreads also remain quite low historically. We experienced a spike in April, but that has come right back down.

This is important because what it means is that investors are rather optimistic going forward. They are willing to accept lower and lower yields, despite the risks. It is a bit like a coiled spring, but certainly in a negative way.

With that, there isn't much margin of safety here, as a spike in spreads would see the underlying portfolio value drop meaningfully. The timing of the event might not be exactly predictable, but as we can see historically, these spikes do occur regularly. The spike in April was relatively mild and rather quick, but it was a bit of a taste. It becomes more important for FTHY the closer we get to the term date.

One of the ways to combat overall risk here for the fund is to invest in a diversified portfolio. It might be virtually all junk-rated, but it is highly diversified throughout many different industry exposures. The fund also lists nearly 300 different holdings, according to CEFConnect.

The top ten holdings don't necessarily make up overly concentrated positions. The largest being 2.39%, which is a bit on the higher end for a high-yield portfolio. With that said, those percentage allocations quickly decline from there for FTHY.

Some other funds that we cover, such as the BNY Mellon High Yield Strategies Fund (DHF), could be a good example of being a bit more diversified. They have listed ~340 holdings on CEFConnect, but none of its holdings have over a 2% allocation, with the sixth largest position already sub-1%.

Conclusion

FTHY provides exposure to a hybrid fixed-income portfolio primarily in high-yield bonds but also carries a sleeve of senior loan exposure. That smaller sleeve can be helpful to balance the fund's borrowing costs for its leverage versus what the underlying portfolio can generate in income. The fund has an attractive distribution yield, though the payout is adjusted fairly regularly and the coverage in terms of income is low—and has been that way since the fund's inception.

With the termination expected in a couple of years, the fund is worth continuing to hold here or monitoring more closely for an entry point. That said, with the current discount, it does not provide an attractive level that one would want to initiate a position.

data.title: Best Dividend Kings: August 2025
data.description:
2025 Review

Collectively, the Dividend Kings saw a modest gain in July of 0.64%, but ultimately they trailed the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY), which posted a solid gain of 2.30%. The Kings are faring much better in August thus far, up 2.94% versus a gain of just 0.55% for SPY. However, year-to-date, the Kings continue to trail SPY, 5.71% to 8.75%.

Despite the lackluster performance in July, 3 more Dividend Kings have pushed ahead of SPY year-to-date, bringing the total to 17.

Best Dividend Kings in 2025

As of August 21st, fifteen Dividend Kings have achieved double-digit total returns this year, with one exceeding a gain of 40%. Here are the best-performing Kings:

  1. National Fuel Gas (NFG) +44.92%
  2. Altria Group (MO) +34.05%
  3. Tootsie Roll (TR) +25.86%
  4. Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) +25.79%
  5. Fortis (FTS) +24.45%
  6. Nucor Corporation (NUE) +23.82%
  7. Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) +23.64%
  8. AbbVie (ABBV) +20.10%
  9. Canadian Utilities Limited (OTCPK:CDUAF) +18.98%
  10. Genuine Parts Company (GPC) +18.68%
  11. Abbott Laboratories (ABT) +17.87%
  12. Parker-Hannifin (PH) +15.85%
  13. Consolidated Edison (ED) +15.75%
  14. The Coca-Cola Company (KO) +15.17%
  15. S&P Global (SPGI) +11.33%

Of the remaining 40 Dividend Kings, 22 have positive single-digit total returns, while 18 are in the red. The worst-performing Dividend King this year is still Target (TGT), down 26.74%.

The five best-performing Dividend Kings in August are:

  1. Lowe's Companies (LOW) +14.35%
  2. Archer-Daniels-Midland +12.81%
  3. AbbVie +10.83%
  4. Becton, Dickinson and Company (BDX) +10.31%
  5. Altria Group +9.27%

Dividend Growth

7 Dividend Kings announced a dividend increase since last month's update.

American States Water

  • Old rate: $0.4655
  • New rate: $0.5040
  • Increase: 8.27%
  • 2025 Growth Rate: 8.26% (comparing 2025 annual payout to the prior year)

Dover Corporation

  • Old rate: $0.5150
  • New rate: $0.5200
  • Increase: 0.97%
  • 2025 Growth Rate: 0.98%

Farmers & Merchants Bancorp

  • Old rate: $9.30
  • New rate: $5.00 (semi-annual dividend switched to quarterly)
  • Increase: 7.53%
  • 2025 Growth Rate: 9.66%

Federal Realty Investment Trust

  • Old rate: $1.10
  • New rate: $1.13
  • Increase: 2.73%
  • 2025 Growth Rate: 1.37%

Illinois Tool Works

  • Old rate: $1.50
  • New rate: $1.61
  • Increase: 7.33%
  • 2025 Growth Rate: 9.12%

Kenvue

  • Old rate: $0.2050
  • New rate: $0.2075
  • Increase: 1.22%
  • 2025 Growth Rate: 1.85%

Stanley Black & Decker

  • Old rate: $0.82
  • New rate: $0.83
  • Increase: 1.22%
  • 2025 Growth Rate: 1.23%

These increases bring the collective 2025 dividend growth rate for all the Dividend Kings up from 5.23% last month to 5.49% today.

For context, in 2024 the Dividend Kings had a 6.32% dividend growth rate. The remaining Dividend Kings will need to provide sizable increases to surpass last year's dividend growth rate.

Earnings Projections

Since last month's update, analyst 3-5 year earnings growth projections have modestly shifted higher, +0.16% on average, for all the Dividend Kings.

We had 4 notable downward revisions.

  • California Water Service Group (CWT) - revised down from -3.73% to -5.07%
  • Kenvue - revised down from 4.28% to 2.26%
  • Nucor Corporation - revised down from 14.40% to 13.10%
  • H2O America (HTO) - revised down from 3.76% to 2.54%

There were also 4 notable upward revisions.

  • Archer-Daniels-Midland - revised up from -0.32% to 2.60%
  • Cincinnati Financial (CINF) - revised up from 1.81% to 4.57%
  • National Fuel Gas - revised up from 20.74% to 22.87%
  • Sysco (SYY) - revised up from 5.81% to 7.17%

Promising Kings in 2025

In last month's update, as of July 21st, I identified 17 Dividend Kings that appeared to be both potentially undervalued and had a strong long-term return estimate. My article was published on July 22nd, and from July 23rd to August 21st, these 17 Kings had an average total return of -0.07%, compared to +0.21% for all 55 Dividend Kings and +0.73% for SPY. Not the best outcome, but these Kings were selected as long-term opportunities.

In total, there were fourteen winners and three losers, a solid performance, and an overall positive return, slightly better than SPY.

As of August 21st, 15 Dividend Kings appear to be both potentially undervalued and have a long-term annualized expected rate of return of at least 10%. Here they are:

ABM 47.86 56.53 -15.34% 2.21% 1.88% 11.72%

No new Kings have popped onto the list, meanwhile 2 Kings have dropped off:

Removed:

  • Abbott Laboratories - 2% undervalued +9.93% ROR

Dividend Yield Theory & Expected Rate of Return

I utilize Dividend Yield Theory as my primary valuation model for this analysis. Here is a brief explanation of how it works as well as my expected rate of return.

Dividend yield theory compares the forward dividend yield to the trailing 5-year average dividend yield to measure undervaluation. The expected long-term annualized rate of return is measured as follows:

Expected ROR = 3-5 year analyst earnings growth forecast * margin of safety + forward dividend yield + 5-year annualized return for fair value based on dividend yield theory

Let's take a closer look at the dividend yield theory valuation for the 15 Dividend Kings on our "promising" list.
